    citation_dict[r['title']] = int(r['total_citations'])
papers_2016 = []
for p in papers:

        filename = p.get('filename', '')

            title = filename.replace('.txt', '')
            text_lc = text.lower()

                papers_2016.append({'title': title, 'citations': citation_dict.get(title, 0)})

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(papers_2016))

[{"_id": "694f5530284b10b11dc0a869", "filename": "A Lived Informatics Model of Personal Informatics.txt", "text": "UBICOMP '15, SEPTEMBER 7–11, 2015, OSAKA, JAPAN

A Lived Informatics Model of Personal Informatics 

Daniel A. Epstein1, An Ping2, James Fogarty1, Sean A. Munson2 
1Computer Science & Engineering, 2Human Centered Design & Engineering 
DUB Group, University of Washington 
{depstein, jfogarty}@cs.washington.edu, {anping, smunson}@uw.edu 

ABSTRACT 
Current  models  of  how  people  use  personal  informatics 
systems are largely based in behavior change goals. They do 
not  adequately  characterize  the  integration  of  self-tracking 
into  everyday  life  by  people  with  varying  goals.  We  build 
upon  prior  work  by  embracing  the  perspective  of  lived 
informatics to propose a new model of personal informatics. 
We examine how lived informatics manifests in the habits of 
self-trackers across a variety of domains, first by surveying 
105, 99, and 83 past and present trackers of physical activity, 
finances, and location and then by interviewing 22 trackers 
regarding their lived informatics experiences. We develop a 
model characterizing tracker processes of deciding to track 
and selecting a tool, elaborate on tool usage during collection, 
integration,  and  reflection  as  components  of  tracking  and 
acting,  and  discuss  the  lapsing  and  potential  resuming  of 
tracking.  We  use  our  model  to  surface  underexplored 
challenges  in  lived  informatics,  thus  identifying  future 
directions for personal informatics design and research.  

Author Keywords 
Lived Informatics; Personal Informatics; Self-Tracking; 
Lapsing; Physical Activity; Finances; Location. 

ACM Classification Keywords 
H.5.m. Information interfaces and presentation (e.g., HCI). 

INTRODUCTION 
Personal informatics, or collecting and reflecting on personal 
information,  has  become  increasingly  prevalent.  Personal 
informatics can serve a goal-driven purpose, such as tracking 
weight loss, increasing physical activity, having a record of 
places  visited,  or  tracking  student  loan  payments.  Beyond 
these goal-driven motivations, self-tracking tools are now a 
part  of  everyday  life,  or  “lived  informatics”,  with  people 
tracking out of curiosity or a fascination with numbers [34]. 
The pervasiveness of self-tracking tools has led researchers 
to  study  people’s  use  of  commercial  tools  for  tracking 
location  [24,30],  finances  [20],  food  [11],  weight  [19,25], 
and  physical  activity  [16,34]  and  to  develop  research 
prototypes to expand our understanding of usage [3,8,12,40]. 

Permission  to  make  digital  or  hard  copies  of  all  or  part  of  this  work  for
personal or classroom use is granted without fee provided that copies are 
not made or distributed for profit or commercial advantage and that copies
bear  this  notice  and  the  full  citation  on  the  first  page.  Copyrights  for
components  of  this  work  owned  by  others  than  ACM  must  be  honored.
Abstracting  with  credit  is  permitted.  To  copy  otherwise,  or  republish,  to
post on servers or to redistribute to lists, requires prior specific permission
and/or a fee. Request permissions from permissions@acm.org. 
UbiComp '15, September 7-11, 2015, Osaka, Japan. 
Copyright 2015 © ACM 978-1-4503-3574-4/15/09...$15.00. 
http://dx.doi.org/10.1145/2750858.2804250 

The first and most commonly used model for understanding 
how  people  use  personal  informatics  tools  is  Li  et  al.’s 
five-stage model [21]. The model characterizes how people 
transition  between  preparation,  collection,  integration, 
reflection, and action, describing the iterative nature of these 
stages  and  the  barriers  that  prevent  transitions.  The  model 
emphasizes an end goal of knowledge supporting behavior 
change.  The  model  has  since  been  expanded  and  clarified, 
dividing  and  combining  stages.  For  goal-oriented  trackers, 
reflection  can  be  divided  into  maintenance  and  discovery 
[22].  For  some  trackers,  engagement  with  and  gaining  an 
understanding  of  personal  data  may  be  the  goal  in  itself, 
causing the action stage to be foregone [41]. Reflection often 
occurs  during  collection  rather  than  being  a  separate  stage 
[6], which leads to an alternative three-stage iterative model 
of configuration, data capture, and feedback [7]. 

However,  research  on  everyday  self-tracking  behavior 
identifies several uses of personal informatics not described 
by  current  models.  For  example,  the  five-stage  model 
describes  self-trackers  switching  tools  to  better  fit  their 
information  needs  [21].  People  also  switch  tools  because 
their  devices  break  [16,34],  they  change  phones  [34],  or 
because 
[34]. 
Self-trackers also fail to sustain the habit or get frustrated and 
give up on tools [11]. Articulating a model that encompasses 
these behaviors can offer better guidance to designers.  

receive  a  new 

recommendation 

they 

To develop this model, we studied self-tracking of a variety 
of behaviors to understand how lived informatics manifests 
in the habits of self-trackers. Specifically, we surveyed 105, 
99,  and  83  past  and  present  trackers  of  physical  activity, 
finances,  and  location  on  their  habits  and  conducted  22 
interviews. We report on how people decide to start tracking 
and pick a tool, how they use that tool, how and why people 
switch  between  tools,  and  why  people  stop  tracking.  We 
develop  an  expanded  model  that  captures  the  practices  of 
self-trackers  motivated  to  track  to  change  behaviors, 
instrumenting an activity, and curiosity. 

Specifically, we find: 
•  In  addition  to  being  motivated  to  track  in  support  of 
behavior change goals, people start tracking to instrument 
an activity or out of pure curiosity. 

•  People’s  motivations  for  tracking  guide  the  selection  of 
tracking  tools.  Behavior-change  trackers  do  substantial 
background  research  and  optimize  a  tool  for  their  goal, 
instrumental  trackers  try  to  maximize  their  benefit  from 
the  tools,  and  curiosity-driven  trackers  often  do  not 
actively consider tool selection. 

731

 
 
 
 
 •  Usage of tools varies by domain and reason for tracking, 
primarily  because  people’s  motivations  are  different  in 
each domain. Behavior change trackers collect and review 
data  frequently  and  are  bothered  by  inaccurate  logs, 
instrumental trackers track when the benefits outweigh the 
effort, and curiosity-driven trackers are motivated by how 
interesting they find the data they are tracking. 

•  People frequently switch tools, and in doing so learn about 

what and how they are interested in tracking.  

•  People  lapse  in  their  tool  use  in  four  different  ways: 
track,  difficulty  managing  upkeep, 

forgetting 
intentionally skipping entries, and suspending tracking. 

to 

These  findings  inform  a  revised  model  of  how  people  use 
self-tracking  tools,  independent  of  original  motivation, 
supplementing  the  stage-based  model  for  behavior-change 
tracking. This informs a set of open design problems: 
•  Designing to resume tracking after a lapse, including how 
to use historical data collected before, or even during, the 
lapse.  For  example,  should  a  food  journal  present 
someone’s  previous  eating  habits  when  they  decide  to 
resume tracking to aid in weight loss? If so, how? 

•  Designing to avoid inertia around a long-used tool that no 
longer  supports  a  self-tracker’s  goal.  For  example,  how 
can a financial tracking tool integrate historical data and 
provide an intuitive interface to a person who has managed 
their finances with a custom spreadsheet for years? 

•  Designing to support people’s various goals and transitions 
between  them.  How can the design of a location-tracking 
application  like  Foursquare  support  someone  who,  upon 
receiving a badge for frequent check-ins, becomes curious 
or  even  concerned  about  how  much  they  spend  at 
restaurants?  Can  a  location  diary  like  Moves  show 
someone who tries the application out of curiosity how it 
can help them achieve their fitness goals? 

BACKGROUND 
This  section  reviews  prior  work  on  models  of  personal 
informatics  use,  research  examining  how  people  use  tools, 
and how that use changes over time. 

Models of Personal Informatics 
Li et al. develop a stage-based model of personal informatics 
systems  and  describe  barriers  people  face  in  each  stage 
(Figure 1) [21]. The model has five stages: 1) preparation, or 
starting to collect data and thinking about what information 
to  record  and  how  to  collect  it;  2)  collection,  or  gathering 
data;  3)  integration,  or  preparing  data  to  reflect  upon; 
4) reflection, or examining and exploring data; and 5) action, 
or  taking  one’s  newfound  understanding  of  themselves  to 
inform changes. Motivated by the Transtheoretical Model of 
Behavior  Change  (TTM)  [32],  Li  et  al.  later  identify  two 
phases  of  reflection,  discovery  and  maintenance,  and  note 
people ask different types of questions in each phase [22]. 

From  studying  practices  of  the  Quantified  Self  movement, 
Choe  et  al.  learn  that  reflection  often  occurs  when  data  is 
captured  [6]  and  propose  a  new  model  for  self-monitoring

args.code:
import json

# Load papers data
papers_file_path = var_functions.query_db:56
papers_file = open(papers_file_path, 'r')
all_papers = json.load(papers_file)
papers_file.close()

# Load citations data
citations_file_path = var_functions.query_db:60
citations_file = open(citations_file_path, 'r')
all_citations = json.load(citations_file)
citations_file.close()

print('Loaded {} papers and {} citation records'.format(len(all_papers), len(all_citations)))

# Create citation lookup dictionary
citation_lookup = {}
for record in all_citations:
    citation_lookup[record['title']] = int(record['total_citations'])

# Find 2016 physical activity papers
papers_2016_pa = []
for paper in all_papers:
    text = paper.get('text', '')
    filename = paper.get('filename', '')
    
    if not filename:
        continue
    
    # Check if paper is from 2016
    if '2016' not in text:
        continue
    
    title = filename.replace('.txt', '')
    text_lower = text.lower()
    title_lower = title.lower()
    
    # Check for physical activity keywords
    keywords = ['physical activity', 'fitness', 'exercise', 'workout']
    
    # Check if in title
    in_title = False
    for keyword in keywords:
        if keyword in title_lower:
            in_title = True
            break
    
    # Count occurrences in text
    keyword_count = 0
    for keyword in keywords:
        keyword_count += text_lower.count(keyword)
    
    # Include if in title or mentioned multiple times
    if in_title or keyword_count >= 3:
        total_citations = citation_lookup.get(title, 0)
        papers_2016_pa.append({'title': title, 'total_citations': total_citations})

print('Found {} papers from 2016 in physical activity domain'.format(len(papers_2016_pa)))

# Sort by citations descending
papers_2016_pa.sort(key=lambda x: x['total_citations'], reverse=True)

# Format results
for paper in papers_2016_pa:
    print('Title: {}'.format(paper['title']))
    print('Total Citations: {}'.format(paper['total_citations']))
    print('---')

result = {
    'total_papers': len(papers_2016_pa),
    'papers': papers_2016_pa
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

result.preview:
[{"_id": "694f5530284b10b11dc0a869", "filename": "A Lived Informatics Model of Personal Informatics.txt", "text": "UBICOMP '15, SEPTEMBER 7–11, 2015, OSAKA, JAPAN

A Lived Informatics Model of Personal Informatics 

Daniel A. Epstein1, An Ping2, James Fogarty1, Sean A. Munson2 
1Computer Science & Engineering, 2Human Centered Design & Engineering 
DUB Group, University of Washington 
{depstein, jfogarty}@cs.washington.edu, {anping, smunson}@uw.edu 

ABSTRACT 
Current  models  of  how  people  use  personal  informatics 
systems are largely based in behavior change goals. They do 
not  adequately  characterize  the  integration  of  self-tracking 
into  everyday  life  by  people  with  varying  goals.  We  build 
upon  prior  work  by  embracing  the  perspective  of  lived 
informatics to propose a new model of personal informatics. 
We examine how lived informatics manifests in the habits of 
self-trackers across a variety of domains, first by surveying 
105, 99, and 83 past and present trackers of physical activity, 
finances, and location and then by interviewing 22 trackers 
regarding their lived informatics experiences. We develop a 
model characterizing tracker processes of deciding to track 
and selecting a tool, elaborate on tool usage during collection, 
integration,  and  reflection  as  components  of  tracking  and 
acting,  and  discuss  the  lapsing  and  potential  resuming  of 
tracking.  We  use  our  model  to  surface  underexplored 
challenges  in  lived  informatics,  thus  identifying  future 
directions for personal informatics design and research.  

Author Keywords 
Lived Informatics; Personal Informatics; Self-Tracking; 
Lapsing; Physical Activity; Finances; Location. 

ACM Classification Keywords 
H.5.m. Information interfaces and presentation (e.g., HCI). 

INTRODUCTION 
Personal informatics, or collecting and reflecting on personal 
information,  has  become  increasingly  prevalent.  Personal 
informatics can serve a goal-driven purpose, such as tracking 
weight loss, increasing physical activity, having a record of 
places  visited,  or  tracking  student  loan  payments.  Beyond 
these goal-driven motivations, self-tracking tools are now a 
part  of  everyday  life,  or  “lived  informatics”,  with  people 
tracking out of curiosity or a fascination with numbers [34]. 
The pervasiveness of self-tracking tools has led researchers 
to  study  people’s  use  of  commercial  tools  for  tracking 
location  [24,30],  finances  [20],  food  [11],  weight  [19,25], 
and  physical  activity  [16,34]  and  to  develop  research 
prototypes to expand our understanding of usage [3,8,12,40]. 

Permission  to  make  digital  or  hard  copies  of  all  or  part  of  this  work  for
personal or classroom use is granted without fee provided that copies are 
not made or distributed for profit or commercial advantage and that copies
bear  this  notice  and  the  full  citation  on  the  first  page.  Copyrights  for
components  of  this  work  owned  by  others  than  ACM  must  be  honored.
Abstracting  with  credit  is  permitted.  To  copy  otherwise,  or  republish,  to
post on servers or to redistribute to lists, requires prior specific permission
and/or a fee. Request permissions from permissions@acm.org. 
UbiComp '15, September 7-11, 2015, Osaka, Japan. 
Copyright 2015 © ACM 978-1-4503-3574-4/15/09...$15.00. 
http://dx.doi.org/10.1145/2750858.2804250 

The first and most commonly used model for understanding 
how  people  use  personal  informatics  tools  is  Li  et  al.’s 
five-stage model [21]. The model characterizes how people 
transition  between  preparation,  collection,  integration, 
reflection, and action, describing the iterative nature of these 
stages  and  the  barriers  that  prevent  transitions.  The  model 
emphasizes an end goal of knowledge supporting behavior 
change.  The  model  has  since  been  expanded  and  clarified, 
dividing  and  combining  stages.  For  goal-oriented  trackers, 
reflection  can  be  divided  into  maintenance  and  discovery 
[22].  For  some  trackers,  engagement  with  and  gaining  an 
understanding  of  personal  data  may  be  the  goal  in  itself, 
causing the action stage to be foregone [41]. Reflection often 
occurs  during  collection  rather  than  being  a  separate  stage 
[6], which leads to an alternative three-stage iterative model 
of configuration, data capture, and feedback [7]. 

However,  research  on  everyday  self-tracking  behavior 
identifies several uses of personal informatics not described 
by  current  models.  For  example,  the  five-stage  model 
describes  self-trackers  switching  tools  to  better  fit  their 
information  needs  [21].  People  also  switch  tools  because 
their  devices  break  [16,34],  they  change  phones  [34],  or 
because 
[34]. 
Self-trackers also fail to sustain the habit or get frustrated and 
give up on tools [11]. Articulating a model that encompasses 
these behaviors can offer better guidance to designers.  

receive  a  new 

recommendation 

they 

To develop this model, we studied self-tracking of a variety 
of behaviors to understand how lived informatics manifests 
in the habits of self-trackers. Specifically, we surveyed 105, 
99,  and  83  past  and  present  trackers  of  physical  activity, 
finances,  and  location  on  their  habits  and  conducted  22 
interviews. We report on how people decide to start tracking 
and pick a tool, how they use that tool, how and why people 
switch  between  tools,  and  why  people  stop  tracking.  We 
develop  an  expanded  model  that  captures  the  practices  of 
self-trackers  motivated  to  track  to  change  behaviors, 
instrumenting an activity, and curiosity. 

Specifically, we find: 
•  In  addition  to  being  motivated  to  track  in  support  of 
behavior change goals, people start tracking to instrument 
an activity or out of pure curiosity. 

•  People’s  motivations  for  tracking  guide  the  selection  of 
tracking  tools.  Behavior-change  trackers  do  substantial 
background  research  and  optimize  a  tool  for  their  goal, 
instrumental  trackers  try  to  maximize  their  benefit  from 
the  tools,  and  curiosity-driven  trackers  often  do  not 
actively consider tool selection. 

731

 
 
 
 
 •  Usage of tools varies by domain and reason for tracking, 
primarily  because  people’s  motivations  are  different  in 
each domain. Behavior change trackers collect and review 
data  frequently  and  are  bothered  by  inaccurate  logs, 
instrumental trackers track when the benefits outweigh the 
effort, and curiosity-driven trackers are motivated by how 
interesting they find the data they are tracking. 

•  People frequently switch tools, and in doing so learn about 

what and how they are interested in tracking.  

•  People  lapse  in  their  tool  use  in  four  different  ways: 
track,  difficulty  managing  upkeep, 

forgetting 
intentionally skipping entries, and suspending tracking. 

to 

These  findings  inform  a  revised  model  of  how  people  use 
self-tracking  tools,  independent  of  original  motivation, 
supplementing  the  stage-based  model  for  behavior-change 
tracking. This informs a set of open design problems: 
•  Designing to resume tracking after a lapse, including how 
to use historical data collected before, or even during, the 
lapse.  For  example,  should  a  food  journal  present 
someone’s  previous  eating  habits  when  they  decide  to 
resume tracking to aid in weight loss? If so, how? 

•  Designing to avoid inertia around a long-used tool that no 
longer  supports  a  self-tracker’s  goal.  For  example,  how 
can a financial tracking tool integrate historical data and 
provide an intuitive interface to a person who has managed 
their finances with a custom spreadsheet for years? 

•  Designing to support people’s various goals and transitions 
between  them.  How can the design of a location-tracking 
application  like  Foursquare  support  someone  who,  upon 
receiving a badge for frequent check-ins, becomes curious 
or  even  concerned  about  how  much  they  spend  at 
restaurants?  Can  a  location  diary  like  Moves  show 
someone who tries the application out of curiosity how it 
can help them achieve their fitness goals? 

BACKGROUND 
This  section  reviews  prior  work  on  models  of  personal 
informatics  use,  research  examining  how  people  use  tools, 
and how that use changes over time. 

Models of Personal Informatics 
Li et al. develop a stage-based model of personal informatics 
systems  and  describe  barriers  people  face  in  each  stage 
(Figure 1) [21]. The model has five stages: 1) preparation, or 
starting to collect data and thinking about what information 
to  record  and  how  to  collect  it;  2)  collection,  or  gathering 
data;  3)  integration,  or  preparing  data  to  reflect  upon; 
4) reflection, or examining and exploring data; and 5) action, 
or  taking  one’s  newfound  understanding  of  themselves  to 
inform changes. Motivated by the Transtheoretical Model of 
Behavior  Change  (TTM)  [32],  Li  et  al.  later  identify  two 
phases  of  reflection,  discovery  and  maintenance,  and  note 
people ask different types of questions in each phase [22]. 

From  studying  practices  of  the  Quantified  Self  movement, 
Choe  et  al.  learn  that  reflection  often  occurs  when  data  is 
captured  [6]  and  propose  a  new  model  for  self-monitoring

DUB Group, University of Washington 
{depstein, jfogarty}@cs.washington.edu, {anping, smunson}@uw.edu 

ABSTRACT 
Current  models  of  how  people  use  personal  informatics 
systems are largely based in behavior change goals. They do 
not  adequately  characterize  the  integration  of  self-tracking 
into  everyday  life  by  people  with  varying  goals.  We  build 
upon  prior  work  by  embracing  the  perspective  of  lived 
informatics to propose a new model of personal informatics. 
We examine how lived informatics manifests in the habits of 
self-trackers across a variety of domains, first by surveying 
105, 99, and 83 past and present trackers of physical activity, 
finances, and location and then by interviewing 22 trackers 
regarding their lived informatics experiences. We develop a 
model characterizing tracker processes of deciding to track 
and selecting a tool, elaborate on tool usage during collection, 
integration,  and  reflection  as  components  of  tracking  and 
acting,  and  discuss  the  lapsing  and  potential  resuming  of 
tracking.  We  use  our  model  to  surface  underexplored 
challenges  in  lived  informatics,  thus  identifying  future 
directions for personal informatics design and research.  

Author Keywords 
Lived Informatics; Personal Informatics; Self-Tracking; 
Lapsing; Physical Activity; Finances; Location. 

ACM Classification Keywords 
H.5.m. Information interfaces and presentation (e.g., HCI). 

INTRODUCTION 
Personal informatics, or collecting and reflecting on personal 
information,  has  become  increasingly  prevalent.  Personal 
informatics can serve a goal-driven purpose, such as tracking 
weight loss, increasing physical activity, having a record of 
places  visited,  or  tracking  student  loan  payments.  Beyond 
these goal-driven motivations, self-tracking tools are now a 
part  of  everyday  life,  or  “lived  informatics”,  with  people 
tracking out of curiosity or a fascination with numbers [34]. 
The pervasiveness of self-tracking tools has led researchers 
to  study  people’s  use  of  commercial  tools  for  tracking 
location  [24,30],  finances  [20],  food  [11],  weight  [19,25], 
and  physical  activity  [16,34]  and  to  develop  research 
prototypes to expand our understanding of usage [3,8,12,40].

Copyright 2015 © ACM 978-1-4503-3574-4/15/09...$15.00. 
http://dx.doi.org/10.1145/2750858.2804250 

The first and most commonly used model for understanding 
how  people  use  personal  informatics  tools  is  Li  et  al.’s 
five-stage model [21]. The model characterizes how people 
transition  between  preparation,  collection,  integration, 
reflection, and action, describing the iterative nature of these 
stages  and  the  barriers  that  prevent  transitions.  The  model 
emphasizes an end goal of knowledge supporting behavior 
change.  The  model  has  since  been  expanded  and  clarified, 
dividing  and  combining  stages.  For  goal-oriented  trackers, 
reflection  can  be  divided  into  maintenance  and  discovery 
[22].  For  some  trackers,  engagement  with  and  gaining  an 
understanding  of  personal  data  may  be  the  goal  in  itself, 
causing the action stage to be foregone [41]. Reflection often 
occurs  during  collection  rather  than  being  a  separate  stage 
[6], which leads to an alternative three-stage iterative model 
of configuration, data capture, and feedback [7]. 

However,  research  on  everyday  self-tracking  behavior 
identifies several uses of personal informatics not described 
by  current  models.  For  example,  the  five-stage  model 
describes  self-trackers  switching  tools  to  better  fit  their 
information  needs  [21].  People  also  switch  tools  because 
their  devices  break  [16,34],  they  change  phones  [34],  or 
because 
[34]. 
Self-trackers also fail to sustain the habit or get frustrated and 
give up on tools [11]. Articulating a model that encompasses 
these behaviors can offer better guidance to designers.  

receive  a  new 

recommendation 

they 

To develop this model, we studied self-tracking of a variety 
of behaviors to understand how lived informatics manifests 
in the habits of self-trackers. Specifically, we surveyed 105, 
99,  and  83  past  and  present  trackers  of  physical  activity, 
finances,  and  location  on  their  habits  and  conducted  22 
interviews. We report on how people decide to start tracking 
and pick a tool, how they use that tool, how and why people 
switch  between  tools,  and  why  people  stop  tracking.  We 
develop  an  expanded  model  that  captures  the  practices  of 
self-trackers  motivated  to  track  to  change  behaviors, 
instrumenting an activity, and curiosity. 

Specifically, we find: 
•  In  addition  to  being  motivated  to  track  in  support  of 
behavior change goals, people start tracking to instrument 
an activity or out of pure curiosity. 

•  People’s  motivations  for  tracking  guide  the  selection  of 
tracking  tools.  Behavior-change  trackers  do  substantial 
background  research  and  optimize  a  tool  for  their  goal, 
instrumental  trackers  try  to  maximize  their  benefit  from 
the  tools,  and  curiosity-driven  trackers  often  do  not 
actively consider tool selection. 

731

 
 
 
 
 •  Usage of tools varies by domain and reason for tracking, 
primarily  because  people’s  motivations  are  different  in 
each domain. Behavior change trackers collect and review 
data  frequently  and  are  bothered  by  inaccurate  logs, 
instrumental trackers track when the benefits outweigh the 
effort, and curiosity-driven trackers are motivated by how 
interesting they find the data they are tracking. 

•  People frequently switch tools, and in doing so learn about 

what and how they are interested in tracking.  

•  People  lapse  in  their  tool  use  in  four  different  ways: 
track,  difficulty  managing  upkeep, 

forgetting 
intentionally skipping entries, and suspending tracking. 

to 

These  findings  inform  a  revised  model  of  how  people  use 
self-tracking  tools,  independent  of  original  motivation, 
supplementing  the  stage-based  model  for  behavior-change 
tracking. This informs a set of open design problems: 
•  Designing to resume tracking after a lapse, including how 
to use historical data collected before, or even during, the 
lapse.  For  example,  should  a  food  journal  present 
someone’s  previous  eating  habits  when  they  decide  to 
resume tracking to aid in weight loss? If so, how? 

•  Designing to avoid inertia around a long-used tool that no 
longer  supports  a  self-tracker’s  goal.  For  example,  how 
can a financial tracking tool integrate historical data and 
provide an intuitive interface to a person who has managed 
their finances with a custom spreadsheet for years? 

•  Designing to support people’s various goals and transitions 
between  them.  How can the design of a location-tracking 
application  like  Foursquare  support  someone  who,  upon 
receiving a badge for frequent check-ins, becomes curious 
or  even  concerned  about  how  much  they  spend  at 
restaurants?  Can  a  location  diary  like  Moves  show 
someone who tries the application out of curiosity how it 
can help them achieve their fitness goals? 

BACKGROUND 
This  section  reviews  prior  work  on  models  of  personal 
informatics  use,  research  examining  how  people  use  tools, 
and how that use changes over time. 

Models of Personal Informatics 
Li et al. develop a stage-based model of personal informatics 
systems  and  describe  barriers  people  face  in  each  stage 
(Figure 1) [21]. The model has five stages: 1) preparation, or 
starting to collect data and thinking about what information 
to  record  and  how  to  collect  it;  2)  collection,  or  gathering 
data;  3)  integration,  or  preparing  data  to  reflect  upon; 
4) reflection, or examining and exploring data; and 5) action, 
or  taking  one’s  newfound  understanding  of  themselves  to 
inform changes. Motivated by the Transtheoretical Model of 
Behavior  Change  (TTM)  [32],  Li  et  al.  later  identify  two 
phases  of  reflection,  discovery  and  maintenance,  and  note 
people ask different types of questions in each phase [22]. 

From  studying  practices  of  the  Quantified  Self  movement, 
Choe  et  al.  learn  that  reflection  often  occurs  when  data  is 
captured  [6]  and  propose  a  new  model  for  self-monitoring

args.code:
import json
import re

# Read papers data from file
with open('/tmp/papers_data.json', 'r') as f:
    papers = json.load(f)

# Read citations data  
with open('/tmp/citations_data.json', 'r') as f:
    citations = json.load(f)

print('Papers:', len(papers))
print('Citations:', len(citations))

# Create citation lookup table
citation_counts = {}
for record in citations:
    citation_counts[record['title']] = int(record['total_citations'])

# Find 2016 physical activity papers
results = []
for paper in papers:
    text = paper.get('text', '')
    if '2016' not in text:
        continue
    
    filename = paper.get('filename', '')
    if not filename:
        continue
        
    title = filename.replace('.txt', '')
    text_lc = text.lower()
    title_lc = title.lower()
    
    # Check if physical activity domain
    keywords = ['physical activity', 'fitness', 'exercise']
    in_title = any(k in title_lc for k in keywords)
    count = sum(text_lc.count(k) for k in keywords)
    
    if in_title or count >= 2:
        citations = citation_counts.get(title, 0)
        results.append({'title': title, 'total_citations': citations})

# Sort by citations
results.sort(key=lambda x: x['total_citations'], reverse=True)

print('Found', len(results), 'papers')
for r in results:
    print(r['title'])
    print(r['total_citations'])
    print('---')

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(results))

args.code:
import json
import os

# Load papers data
papers_file_path = var_functions.query_db:94
with open(papers_file_path, 'r') as f:
    all_papers = json.load(f)

print('Total papers in database:', len(all_papers))

# Look for 2016 physical activity papers
papers_2016_physical_activity = []

for paper in all_papers:
    filename = paper.get('filename', '')
    text = paper.get('text', '')
    
    if not filename:
        continue
    
    title = filename.replace('.txt', '')
    
    # Check if from 2016
    if '2016' not in text:
        continue
    
    # Check if physical activity domain
    text_lower = text.lower()
    title_lower = title.lower()
    
    # Check for physical activity keywords
    keywords = ['physical activity', 'fitness', 'exercise', 'workout', 'step count', 'activity tracking']
    
    in_title = any(k in title_lower for k in keywords)
    count_in_text = sum(text_lower.count(k) for k in keywords)
    
    if in_title or count_in_text >= 2:
        papers_2016_physical_activity.append(title)

print('Found', len(papers_2016_physical_activity), 'papers from 2016 in physical activity domain')
print('Sample titles:')
for title in papers_2016_physical_activity[:10]:
    print('  -', title[:80])

result = {'titles': papers_2016_physical_activity, 'count': len(papers_2016_physical_activity)}
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

val_args.code:
import json
import os

# Load papers data
papers_file_path = var_functions.query_db:94
with open(papers_file_path, 'r') as f:
    all_papers = json.load(f)

print('Total papers in database:', len(all_papers))

# Look for 2016 physical activity papers
papers_2016_physical_activity = []

for paper in all_papers:
    filename = paper.get('filename', '')
    text = paper.get('text', '')
    
    if not filename:
        continue
    
    title = filename.replace('.txt', '')
    
    # Check if from 2016
    if '2016' not in text:
        continue
    
    # Check if physical activity domain
    text_lower = text.lower()
    title_lower = title.lower()
    
    # Check for physical activity keywords
    keywords = ['physical activity', 'fitness', 'exercise', 'workout', 'step count', 'activity tracking']
    
    in_title = any(k in title_lower for k in keywords)
    count_in_text = sum(text_lower.count(k) for k in keywords)
    
    if in_title or count_in_text >= 2:
        papers_2016_physical_activity.append(title)

print('Found', len(papers_2016_physical_activity), 'papers from 2016 in physical activity domain')
print('Sample titles:')
for title in papers_2016_physical_activity[:10]:
    print('  -', title[:80])

result = {'titles': papers_2016_physical_activity, 'count': len(papers_2016_physical_activity)}
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

args.code:
import json

# Load papers from the full dataset
papers_path = var_functions.query_db:94
with open(papers_path, 'r') as f:
    all_papers = json.load(f)

# Load citation totals
citations_path = var_functions.query_db:100
with open(citations_path, 'r') as f:
    all_citations = json.load(f)

# Create citation lookup table
citation_lookup = {item['title']: int(item['total_citations']) for item in all_citations}

# Find papers from 2016 in physical activity domain
papers_2016_pa = []
for paper in all_papers:
    filename = paper.get('filename', '')
    text = paper.get('text', '')
    
    if not filename:
        continue
    
    title = filename.replace('.txt', '')
    
    # Check if from 2016
    if '2016' not in text:
        continue
    
    # Check for physical activity keywords
    text_lower = text.lower()
    title_lower = title.lower()
    keywords = ['physical activity', 'fitness', 'exercise', 'workout']
    
    in_title = any(k in title_lower for k in keywords)
    keyword_count = sum(text_lower.count(k) for k in keywords)
    
    if in_title or keyword_count >= 3:
        total_citations = citation_lookup.get(title, 0)
        papers_2016_pa.append({'title': title, 'total_citations': total_citations})

# Sort by citations descending
papers_2016_pa.sort(key=lambda x: x['total_citations'], reverse=True)

# Generate output
output_lines = []
output_lines.append(f"Found {len(papers_2016_pa)} papers from 2016 in physical activity domain:")
output_lines.append("")

for paper in papers_2016_pa:
    output_lines.append(f"Title: {paper['title']}")
    output_lines.append(f"Total Citations: {paper['total_citations']}")
    output_lines.append("")

result_text = "\n".join(output_lines)
print('__RESULT__:')
print(result_text)

[{"_id": "694f5530284b10b11dc0a869", "filename": "A Lived Informatics Model of Personal Informatics.txt", "text": "UBICOMP '15, SEPTEMBER 7–11, 2015, OSAKA, JAPAN

A Lived Informatics Model of Personal Informatics 

Daniel A. Epstein1, An Ping2, James Fogarty1, Sean A. Munson2 
1Computer Science & Engineering, 2Human Centered Design & Engineering 
DUB Group, University of Washington 
{depstein, jfogarty}@cs.washington.edu, {anping, smunson}@uw.edu 

ABSTRACT 
Current  models  of  how  people  use  personal  informatics 
systems are largely based in behavior change goals. They do 
not  adequately  characterize  the  integration  of  self-tracking 
into  everyday  life  by  people  with  varying  goals.  We  build 
upon  prior  work  by  embracing  the  perspective  of  lived 
informatics to propose a new model of personal informatics. 
We examine how lived informatics manifests in the habits of 
self-trackers across a variety of domains, first by surveying 
105, 99, and 83 past and present trackers of physical activity, 
finances, and location and then by interviewing 22 trackers 
regarding their lived informatics experiences. We develop a 
model characterizing tracker processes of deciding to track 
and selecting a tool, elaborate on tool usage during collection, 
integration,  and  reflection  as  components  of  tracking  and 
acting,  and  discuss  the  lapsing  and  potential  resuming  of 
tracking.  We  use  our  model  to  surface  underexplored 
challenges  in  lived  informatics,  thus  identifying  future 
directions for personal informatics design and research.  

Author Keywords 
Lived Informatics; Personal Informatics; Self-Tracking; 
Lapsing; Physical Activity; Finances; Location. 

ACM Classification Keywords 
H.5.m. Information interfaces and presentation (e.g., HCI). 

INTRODUCTION 
Personal informatics, or collecting and reflecting on personal 
information,  has  become  increasingly  prevalent.  Personal 
informatics can serve a goal-driven purpose, such as tracking 
weight loss, increasing physical activity, having a record of 
places  visited,  or  tracking  student  loan  payments.  Beyond 
these goal-driven motivations, self-tracking tools are now a 
part  of  everyday  life,  or  “lived  informatics”,  with  people 
tracking out of curiosity or a fascination with numbers [34]. 
The pervasiveness of self-tracking tools has led researchers 
to  study  people’s  use  of  commercial  tools  for  tracking 
location  [24,30],  finances  [20],  food  [11],  weight  [19,25], 
and  physical  activity  [16,34]  and  to  develop  research 
prototypes to expand our understanding of usage [3,8,12,40]. 

Permission  to  make  digital  or  hard  copies  of  all  or  part  of  this  work  for
personal or classroom use is granted without fee provided that copies are 
not made or distributed for profit or commercial advantage and that copies
bear  this  notice  and  the  full  citation  on  the  first  page.  Copyrights  for
components  of  this  work  owned  by  others  than  ACM  must  be  honored.
Abstracting  with  credit  is  permitted.  To  copy  otherwise,  or  republish,  to
post on servers or to redistribute to lists, requires prior specific permission
and/or a fee. Request permissions from permissions@acm.org. 
UbiComp '15, September 7-11, 2015, Osaka, Japan. 
Copyright 2015 © ACM 978-1-4503-3574-4/15/09...$15.00. 
http://dx.doi.org/10.1145/2750858.2804250 

The first and most commonly used model for understanding 
how  people  use  personal  informatics  tools  is  Li  et  al.’s 
five-stage model [21]. The model characterizes how people 
transition  between  preparation,  collection,  integration, 
reflection, and action, describing the iterative nature of these 
stages  and  the  barriers  that  prevent  transitions.  The  model 
emphasizes an end goal of knowledge supporting behavior 
change.  The  model  has  since  been  expanded  and  clarified, 
dividing  and  combining  stages.  For  goal-oriented  trackers, 
reflection  can  be  divided  into  maintenance  and  discovery 
[22].  For  some  trackers,  engagement  with  and  gaining  an 
understanding  of  personal  data  may  be  the  goal  in  itself, 
causing the action stage to be foregone [41]. Reflection often 
occurs  during  collection  rather  than  being  a  separate  stage 
[6], which leads to an alternative three-stage iterative model 
of configuration, data capture, and feedback [7]. 

However,  research  on  everyday  self-tracking  behavior 
identifies several uses of personal informatics not described 
by  current  models.  For  example,  the  five-stage  model 
describes  self-trackers  switching  tools  to  better  fit  their 
information  needs  [21].  People  also  switch  tools  because 
their  devices  break  [16,34],  they  change  phones  [34],  or 
because 
[34]. 
Self-trackers also fail to sustain the habit or get frustrated and 
give up on tools [11]. Articulating a model that encompasses 
these behaviors can offer better guidance to designers.  

receive  a  new 

recommendation 

they 

To develop this model, we studied self-tracking of a variety 
of behaviors to understand how lived informatics manifests 
in the habits of self-trackers. Specifically, we surveyed 105, 
99,  and  83  past  and  present  trackers  of  physical  activity, 
finances,  and  location  on  their  habits  and  conducted  22 
interviews. We report on how people decide to start tracking 
and pick a tool, how they use that tool, how and why people 
switch  between  tools,  and  why  people  stop  tracking.  We 
develop  an  expanded  model  that  captures  the  practices  of 
self-trackers  motivated  to  track  to  change  behaviors, 
instrumenting an activity, and curiosity. 

Specifically, we find: 
•  In  addition  to  being  motivated  to  track  in  support  of 
behavior change goals, people start tracking to instrument 
an activity or out of pure curiosity. 

•  People’s  motivations  for  tracking  guide  the  selection  of 
tracking  tools.  Behavior-change  trackers  do  substantial 
background  research  and  optimize  a  tool  for  their  goal, 
instrumental  trackers  try  to  maximize  their  benefit  from 
the  tools,  and  curiosity-driven  trackers  often  do  not 
actively consider tool selection. 

731

 
 
 
 
 •  Usage of tools varies by domain and reason for tracking, 
primarily  because  people’s  motivations  are  different  in 
each domain. Behavior change trackers collect and review 
data  frequently  and  are  bothered  by  inaccurate  logs, 
instrumental trackers track when the benefits outweigh the 
effort, and curiosity-driven trackers are motivated by how 
interesting they find the data they are tracking. 

•  People frequently switch tools, and in doing so learn about 

what and how they are interested in tracking.  

•  People  lapse  in  their  tool  use  in  four  different  ways: 
track,  difficulty  managing  upkeep, 

forgetting 
intentionally skipping entries, and suspending tracking. 

to 

These  findings  inform  a  revised  model  of  how  people  use 
self-tracking  tools,  independent  of  original  motivation, 
supplementing  the  stage-based  model  for  behavior-change 
tracking. This informs a set of open design problems: 
•  Designing to resume tracking after a lapse, including how 
to use historical data collected before, or even during, the 
lapse.  For  example,  should  a  food  journal  present 
someone’s  previous  eating  habits  when  they  decide  to 
resume tracking to aid in weight loss? If so, how? 

•  Designing to avoid inertia around a long-used tool that no 
longer  supports  a  self-tracker’s  goal.  For  example,  how 
can a financial tracking tool integrate historical data and 
provide an intuitive interface to a person who has managed 
their finances with a custom spreadsheet for years? 

•  Designing to support people’s various goals and transitions 
between  them.  How can the design of a location-tracking 
application  like  Foursquare  support  someone  who,  upon 
receiving a badge for frequent check-ins, becomes curious 
or  even  concerned  about  how  much  they  spend  at 
restaurants?  Can  a  location  diary  like  Moves  show 
someone who tries the application out of curiosity how it 
can help them achieve their fitness goals? 

BACKGROUND 
This  section  reviews  prior  work  on  models  of  personal 
informatics  use,  research  examining  how  people  use  tools, 
and how that use changes over time. 

Models of Personal Informatics 
Li et al. develop a stage-based model of personal informatics 
systems  and  describe  barriers  people  face  in  each  stage 
(Figure 1) [21]. The model has five stages: 1) preparation, or 
starting to collect data and thinking about what information 
to  record  and  how  to  collect  it;  2)  collection,  or  gathering 
data;  3)  integration,  or  preparing  data  to  reflect  upon; 
4) reflection, or examining and exploring data; and 5) action, 
or  taking  one’s  newfound  understanding  of  themselves  to 
inform changes. Motivated by the Transtheoretical Model of 
Behavior  Change  (TTM)  [32],  Li  et  al.  later  identify  two 
phases  of  reflection,  discovery  and  maintenance,  and  note 
people ask different types of questions in each phase [22]. 

From  studying  practices  of  the  Quantified  Self  movement, 
Choe  et  al.  learn  that  reflection  often  occurs  when  data  is 
captured  [6]  and  propose  a  new  model  for  self-monitoring

args.code:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import json
import re

# Read papers from the data file
papers_file = open('/workspace/paper_docs_results.json')
papers = json.load(papers_file)
papers_file.close()

# Read citations from the data file
citations_file = open('/workspace/citations_results.json')
citations = json.load(citations_file)
citations_file.close()

print("Total papers loaded:", len(papers))
print("Total citation records loaded:", len(citations))

# Create citation lookup dictionary
citation_dict = {}
for record in citations:
    citation_dict[record['title']] = int(record['total_citations'])

# Find 2016 physical activity papers
papers_2016_pa = []
for paper in papers:
    filename = paper.get('filename', '')
    text = paper.get('text', '')
    
    if not filename:
        continue
    
    # Extract title from filename
    title = filename.replace('.txt', '')
    
    # Check if 2016 appears in the text
    if '2016' not in text:
        continue
    
    # Check domain
    text_lower = text.lower()
    title_lower = title.lower()
    
    # Keywords for physical activity domain
    keywords = ['physical activity', 'fitness', 'exercise', 'workout']
    
    # Check if keywords in title
    in_title = any(kw in title_lower for kw in keywords)
    
    # Count occurrences in text
    keyword_count = 0
    for kw in keywords:
        keyword_count += text_lower.count(kw)
    
    # If keywords in title or mentioned at least 3 times in text
    if in_title or keyword_count >= 3:
        total_citations = citation_dict.get(title, 0)
        papers_2016_pa.append({'title': title, 'total_citations': total_citations})

# Sort by citations descending
papers_2016_pa.sort(key=lambda x: x['total_citations'], reverse=True)

# Generate final output
output_lines = []
output_lines.append("=" * 60)
output_lines.append("PAPERS FROM 2016 IN PHYSICAL ACTIVITY DOMAIN")
output_lines.append("=" * 60)
output_lines.append("Found {} papers".format(len(papers_2016_pa)))
output_lines.append("")

for paper in papers_2016_pa:
    output_lines.append("Title: {}".format(paper['title']))
    output_lines.append("Total Citations: {}".format(paper['total_citations']))
    output_lines.append("")

result_text = "\n".join(output_lines)
print("__RESULT__:")
print(result_text)

    keywords = ['physical activity', 'fitness', 'exercise', 'workout']
    
    # Check if keywords in title
    in_title = any(kw in title_lower for kw in keywords)
    
    # Count occurrences in text
    keyword_count = 0
    for kw in keywords:
        keyword_count += text_lower.count(kw)
    
    # If keywords in title or mentioned at least 3 times in text
    if in_title or keyword_count >= 3:
        total_citations = citation_dict.get(title, 0)
        papers_2016_pa.append({'title': title, 'total_citations': total_citations})

# Sort by citations descending
papers_2016_pa.sort(key=lambda x: x['total_citations'], reverse=True)

# Generate final output
output_lines = []
output_lines.append("=" * 60)
output_lines.append("PAPERS FROM 2016 IN PHYSICAL ACTIVITY DOMAIN")
output_lines.append("=" * 60)
output_lines.append("Found {} papers".format(len(papers_2016_pa)))
output_lines.append("")

for paper in papers_2016_pa:
    output_lines.append("Title: {}".format(paper['title']))
    output_lines.append("Total Citations: {}".format(paper['total_citations']))
    output_lines.append("")

result_text = "\n".join(output_lines)
print("__RESULT__:")
print(result_text)

[{"_id": "694f5530284b10b11dc0a869", "filename": "A Lived Informatics Model of Personal Informatics.txt", "text": "UBICOMP '15, SEPTEMBER 7–11, 2015, OSAKA, JAPAN

A Lived Informatics Model of Personal Informatics 

Daniel A. Epstein1, An Ping2, James Fogarty1, Sean A. Munson2 
1Computer Science & Engineering, 2Human Centered Design & Engineering 
DUB Group, University of Washington 
{depstein, jfogarty}@cs.washington.edu, {anping, smunson}@uw.edu 

ABSTRACT 
Current  models  of  how  people  use  personal  informatics 
systems are largely based in behavior change goals. They do 
not  adequately  characterize  the  integration  of  self-tracking 
into  everyday  life  by  people  with  varying  goals.  We  build 
upon  prior  work  by  embracing  the  perspective  of  lived 
informatics to propose a new model of personal informatics. 
We examine how lived informatics manifests in the habits of 
self-trackers across a variety of domains, first by surveying 
105, 99, and 83 past and present trackers of physical activity, 
finances, and location and then by interviewing 22 trackers 
regarding their lived informatics experiences. We develop a 
model characterizing tracker processes of deciding to track 
and selecting a tool, elaborate on tool usage during collection, 
integration,  and  reflection  as  components  of  tracking  and 
acting,  and  discuss  the  lapsing  and  potential  resuming  of 
tracking.  We  use  our  model  to  surface  underexplored 
challenges  in  lived  informatics,  thus  identifying  future 
directions for personal informatics design and research.  

Author Keywords 
Lived Informatics; Personal Informatics; Self-Tracking; 
Lapsing; Physical Activity; Finances; Location. 

ACM Classification Keywords 
H.5.m. Information interfaces and presentation (e.g., HCI). 

INTRODUCTION 
Personal informatics, or collecting and reflecting on personal 
information,  has  become  increasingly  prevalent.  Personal 
informatics can serve a goal-driven purpose, such as tracking 
weight loss, increasing physical activity, having a record of 
places  visited,  or  tracking  student  loan  payments.  Beyond 
these goal-driven motivations, self-tracking tools are now a 
part  of  everyday  life,  or  “lived  informatics”,  with  people 
tracking out of curiosity or a fascination with numbers [34]. 
The pervasiveness of self-tracking tools has led researchers 
to  study  people’s  use  of  commercial  tools  for  tracking 
location  [24,30],  finances  [20],  food  [11],  weight  [19,25], 
and  physical  activity  [16,34]  and  to  develop  research 
prototypes to expand our understanding of usage [3,8,12,40].

http://dx.doi.org/10.1145/2750858.2804250 

The first and most commonly used model for understanding 
how  people  use  personal  informatics  tools  is  Li  et  al.’s 
five-stage model [21]. The model characterizes how people 
transition  between  preparation,  collection,  integration, 
reflection, and action, describing the iterative nature of these 
stages  and  the  barriers  that  prevent  transitions.  The  model 
emphasizes an end goal of knowledge supporting behavior 
change.  The  model  has  since  been  expanded  and  clarified, 
dividing  and  combining  stages.  For  goal-oriented  trackers, 
reflection  can  be  divided  into  maintenance  and  discovery 
[22].  For  some  trackers,  engagement  with  and  gaining  an 
understanding  of  personal  data  may  be  the  goal  in  itself, 
causing the action stage to be foregone [41]. Reflection often 
occurs  during  collection  rather  than  being  a  separate  stage 
[6], which leads to an alternative three-stage iterative model 
of configuration, data capture, and feedback [7]. 

However,  research  on  everyday  self-tracking  behavior 
identifies several uses of personal informatics not described 
by  current  models.  For  example,  the  five-stage  model 
describes  self-trackers  switching  tools  to  better  fit  their 
information  needs  [21].  People  also  switch  tools  because 
their  devices  break  [16,34],  they  change  phones  [34],  or 
because 
[34]. 
Self-trackers also fail to sustain the habit or get frustrated and 
give up on tools [11]. Articulating a model that encompasses 
these behaviors can offer better guidance to designers.

they 

To develop this model, we studied self-tracking of a variety 
of behaviors to understand how lived informatics manifests 
in the habits of self-trackers. Specifically, we surveyed 105, 
99,  and  83  past  and  present  trackers  of  physical  activity, 
finances,  and  location  on  their  habits  and  conducted  22 
interviews. We report on how people decide to start tracking 
and pick a tool, how they use that tool, how and why people 
switch  between  tools,  and  why  people  stop  tracking.  We 
develop  an  expanded  model  that  captures  the  practices  of 
self-trackers  motivated  to  track  to  change  behaviors, 
instrumenting an activity, and curiosity. 

Specifically, we find: 
•  In  addition  to  being  motivated  to  track  in  support  of 
behavior change goals, people start tracking to instrument 
an activity or out of pure curiosity. 

•  People’s  motivations  for  tracking  guide  the  selection  of 
tracking  tools.  Behavior-change  trackers  do  substantial 
background  research  and  optimize  a  tool  for  their  goal, 
instrumental  trackers  try  to  maximize  their  benefit  from 
the  tools,  and  curiosity-driven  trackers  often  do  not 
actively consider tool selection. 

731

 
 
 
 
 •  Usage of tools varies by domain and reason for tracking, 
primarily  because  people’s  motivations  are  different  in 
each domain. Behavior change trackers collect and review 
data  frequently  and  are  bothered  by  inaccurate  logs, 
instrumental trackers track when the benefits outweigh the 
effort, and curiosity-driven trackers are motivated by how 
interesting they find the data they are tracking. 

•  People frequently switch tools, and in doing so learn about 

what and how they are interested in tracking.  

•  People  lapse  in  their  tool  use  in  four  different  ways: 
track,  difficulty  managing  upkeep, 

forgetting 
intentionally skipping entries, and suspending tracking. 

to 

These  findings  inform  a  revised  model  of  how  people  use 
self-tracking  tools,  independent  of  original  motivation, 
supplementing  the  stage-based  model  for  behavior-change 
tracking. This informs a set of open design problems: 
•  Designing to resume tracking after a lapse, including how 
to use historical data collected before, or even during, the 
lapse.  For  example,  should  a  food  journal  present 
someone’s  previous  eating  habits  when  they  decide  to 
resume tracking to aid in weight loss? If so, how? 

•  Designing to avoid inertia around a long-used tool that no 
longer  supports  a  self-tracker’s  goal.  For  example,  how 
can a financial tracking tool integrate historical data and 
provide an intuitive interface to a person who has managed 
their finances with a custom spreadsheet for years? 

•  Designing to support people’s various goals and transitions 
between  them.  How can the design of a location-tracking 
application  like  Foursquare  support  someone  who,  upon 
receiving a badge for frequent check-ins, becomes curious 
or  even  concerned  about  how  much  they  spend  at 
restaurants?  Can  a  location  diary  like  Moves  show 
someone who tries the application out of curiosity how it 
can help them achieve their fitness goals? 

BACKGROUND 
This  section  reviews  prior  work  on  models  of  personal 
informatics  use,  research  examining  how  people  use  tools, 
and how that use changes over time. 

Models of Personal Informatics 
Li et al. develop a stage-based model of personal informatics 
systems  and  describe  barriers  people  face  in  each  stage 
(Figure 1) [21]. The model has five stages: 1) preparation, or 
starting to collect data and thinking about what information 
to  record  and  how  to  collect  it;  2)  collection,  or  gathering 
data;  3)  integration,  or  preparing  data  to  reflect  upon; 
4) reflection, or examining and exploring data; and 5) action, 
or  taking  one’s  newfound  understanding  of  themselves  to 
inform changes. Motivated by the Transtheoretical Model of 
Behavior  Change  (TTM)  [32],  Li  et  al.  later  identify  two 
phases  of  reflection,  discovery  and  maintenance,  and  note 
people ask different types of questions in each phase [22]. 

From  studying  practices  of  the  Quantified  Self  movement, 
Choe  et  al.  learn  that  reflection  often  occurs  when  data  is 
captured  [6]  and  propose  a  new  model  for  self-monitoring

args.code:
import json
import os

# Get file paths from the variables
papers_file = var_functions.query_db:118
citations_file = var_functions.query_db:100

print("=== LOADING DATA ===")
print("Papers file:", papers_file)
print("Citations file:", citations_file)

# Load papers data
with open(papers_file, 'r') as f:
    papers = json.load(f)

# Load citations data  
with open(citations_file, 'r') as f:
    citations = json.load(f)

print(f"\nLoaded {len(papers)} papers and {len(citations)} citation records")

# Create citation lookup dictionary
citation_dict = {}
for record in citations:
    citation_dict[record['title']] = int(record['total_citations'])

# Find 2016 physical activity papers
papers_2016_pa = []

for paper in papers:
    text = paper.get('text', '')
    filename = paper.get('filename', '')
    
    if not filename:
        continue
    
    # Check if from 2016
    if '2016' not in text:
        continue
    
    title = filename.replace('.txt', '')
    text_lc = text.lower()
    title_lc = title.lower()
    
    # Check physical activity keywords
    keywords = ['physical activity', 'fitness', 'exercise', 'workout']
    
    in_title = any(k in title_lc for k in keywords)
    count_in_text = sum(text_lc.count(k) for k in keywords)
    
    if in_title or count_in_text >= 3:
        total_citations = citation_dict.get(title, 0)
        papers_2016_pa.append({'title': title, 'total_citations': total_citations})

# Sort by citations descending
papers_2016_pa.sort(key=lambda x: x['total_citations'], reverse=True)

print(f"\n=== RESULTS ===")
print(f"Found {len(papers_2016_pa)} papers from 2016 in physical activity domain")
print()

# Format output
output_lines = []
for paper in papers_2016_pa:
    output_lines.append(f"Title: {paper['title']}")
    output_lines.append(f"Total Citations: {paper['total_citations']}")
    output_lines.append("-" * 50)

result_text = "\n".join(output_lines)
print(result_text)

# Save to file for return
with open('/workspace/final_results.json', 'w') as f:
    json.dump(papers_2016_pa, f)

print("\n=== SUMMARY ===")
print(f"Total papers found: {len(papers_2016_pa)}")

val_args.code:
import json
import os

# Get file paths from the variables
papers_file = var_functions.query_db:118
citations_file = var_functions.query_db:100

print("=== LOADING DATA ===")
print("Papers file:", papers_file)
print("Citations file:", citations_file)

# Load papers data
with open(papers_file, 'r') as f:
    papers = json.load(f)

# Load citations data  
with open(citations_file, 'r') as f:
    citations = json.load(f)

print(f"\nLoaded {len(papers)} papers and {len(citations)} citation records")

# Create citation lookup dictionary
citation_dict = {}
for record in citations:
    citation_dict[record['title']] = int(record['total_citations'])

# Find 2016 physical activity papers
papers_2016_pa = []

for paper in papers:
    text = paper.get('text', '')
    filename = paper.get('filename', '')
    
    if not filename:
        continue
    
    # Check if from 2016
    if '2016' not in text:
        continue
    
    title = filename.replace('.txt', '')
    text_lc = text.lower()
    title_lc = title.lower()
    
    # Check physical activity keywords
    keywords = ['physical activity', 'fitness', 'exercise', 'workout']
    
    in_title = any(k in title_lc for k in keywords)
    count_in_text = sum(text_lc.count(k) for k in keywords)
    
    if in_title or count_in_text >= 3:
        total_citations = citation_dict.get(title, 0)
        papers_2016_pa.append({'title': title, 'total_citations': total_citations})

# Sort by citations descending
papers_2016_pa.sort(key=lambda x: x['total_citations'], reverse=True)

print(f"\n=== RESULTS ===")
print(f"Found {len(papers_2016_pa)} papers from 2016 in physical activity domain")
print()

# Format output
output_lines = []
for paper in papers_2016_pa:
    output_lines.append(f"Title: {paper['title']}")
    output_lines.append(f"Total Citations: {paper['total_citations']}")
    output_lines.append("-" * 50)

result_text = "\n".join(output_lines)
print(result_text)

# Save to file for return
with open('/workspace/final_results.json', 'w') as f:
    json.dump(papers_2016_pa, f)

print("\n=== SUMMARY ===")
print(f"Total papers found: {len(papers_2016_pa)}")

[{"_id": "694f5530284b10b11dc0a869", "filename": "A Lived Informatics Model of Personal Informatics.txt", "text": "UBICOMP '15, SEPTEMBER 7–11, 2015, OSAKA, JAPAN

A Lived Informatics Model of Personal Informatics 

Daniel A. Epstein1, An Ping2, James Fogarty1, Sean A. Munson2 
1Computer Science & Engineering, 2Human Centered Design & Engineering 
DUB Group, University of Washington 
{depstein, jfogarty}@cs.washington.edu, {anping, smunson}@uw.edu 

ABSTRACT 
Current  models  of  how  people  use  personal  informatics 
systems are largely based in behavior change goals. They do 
not  adequately  characterize  the  integration  of  self-tracking 
into  everyday  life  by  people  with  varying  goals.  We  build 
upon  prior  work  by  embracing  the  perspective  of  lived 
informatics to propose a new model of personal informatics. 
We examine how lived informatics manifests in the habits of 
self-trackers across a variety of domains, first by surveying 
105, 99, and 83 past and present trackers of physical activity, 
finances, and location and then by interviewing 22 trackers 
regarding their lived informatics experiences. We develop a 
model characterizing tracker processes of deciding to track 
and selecting a tool, elaborate on tool usage during collection, 
integration,  and  reflection  as  components  of  tracking  and 
acting,  and  discuss  the  lapsing  and  potential  resuming  of 
tracking.  We  use  our  model  to  surface  underexplored 
challenges  in  lived  informatics,  thus  identifying  future 
directions for personal informatics design and research.  

Author Keywords 
Lived Informatics; Personal Informatics; Self-Tracking; 
Lapsing; Physical Activity; Finances; Location. 

ACM Classification Keywords 
H.5.m. Information interfaces and presentation (e.g., HCI). 

INTRODUCTION 
Personal informatics, or collecting and reflecting on personal 
information,  has  become  increasingly  prevalent.  Personal 
informatics can serve a goal-driven purpose, such as tracking 
weight loss, increasing physical activity, having a record of 
places  visited,  or  tracking  student  loan  payments.  Beyond 
these goal-driven motivations, self-tracking tools are now a 
part  of  everyday  life,  or  “lived  informatics”,  with  people 
tracking out of curiosity or a fascination with numbers [34]. 
The pervasiveness of self-tracking tools has led researchers 
to  study  people’s  use  of  commercial  tools  for  tracking 
location  [24,30],  finances  [20],  food  [11],  weight  [19,25], 
and  physical  activity  [16,34]  and  to  develop  research 
prototypes to expand our understanding of usage [3,8,12,40]. 

Permission  to  make  digital  or  hard  copies  of  all  or  part  of  this  work  for
personal or classroom use is granted without fee provided that copies are 
not made or distributed for profit or commercial advantage and that copies
bear  this  notice  and  the  full  citation  on  the  first  page.  Copyrights  for
components  of  this  work  owned  by  others  than  ACM  must  be  honored.
Abstracting  with  credit  is  permitted.  To  copy  otherwise,  or  republish,  to
post on servers or to redistribute to lists, requires prior specific permission
and/or a fee. Request permissions from permissions@acm.org. 
UbiComp '15, September 7-11, 2015, Osaka, Japan. 
Copyright 2015 © ACM 978-1-4503-3574-4/15/09...$15.00. 
http://dx.doi.org/10.1145/2750858.2804250 

The first and most commonly used model for understanding 
how  people  use  personal  informatics  tools  is  Li  et  al.’s 
five-stage model [21]. The model characterizes how people 
transition  between  preparation,  collection,  integration, 
reflection, and action, describing the iterative nature of these 
stages  and  the  barriers  that  prevent  transitions.  The  model 
emphasizes an end goal of knowledge supporting behavior 
change.  The  model  has  since  been  expanded  and  clarified, 
dividing  and  combining  stages.  For  goal-oriented  trackers, 
reflection  can  be  divided  into  maintenance  and  discovery 
[22].  For  some  trackers,  engagement  with  and  gaining  an 
understanding  of  personal  data  may  be  the  goal  in  itself, 
causing the action stage to be foregone [41]. Reflection often 
occurs  during  collection  rather  than  being  a  separate  stage 
[6], which leads to an alternative three-stage iterative model 
of configuration, data capture, and feedback [7]. 

However,  research  on  everyday  self-tracking  behavior 
identifies several uses of personal informatics not described 
by  current  models.  For  example,  the  five-stage  model 
describes  self-trackers  switching  tools  to  better  fit  their 
information  needs  [21].  People  also  switch  tools  because 
their  devices  break  [16,34],  they  change  phones  [34],  or 
because 
[34]. 
Self-trackers also fail to sustain the habit or get frustrated and 
give up on tools [11]. Articulating a model that encompasses 
these behaviors can offer better guidance to designers.  

receive  a  new 

recommendation 

they 

To develop this model, we studied self-tracking of a variety 
of behaviors to understand how lived informatics manifests 
in the habits of self-trackers. Specifically, we surveyed 105, 
99,  and  83  past  and  present  trackers  of  physical  activity, 
finances,  and  location  on  their  habits  and  conducted  22 
interviews. We report on how people decide to start tracking 
and pick a tool, how they use that tool, how and why people 
switch  between  tools,  and  why  people  stop  tracking.  We 
develop  an  expanded  model  that  captures  the  practices  of 
self-trackers  motivated  to  track  to  change  behaviors, 
instrumenting an activity, and curiosity. 

Specifically, we find: 
•  In  addition  to  being  motivated  to  track  in  support  of 
behavior change goals, people start tracking to instrument 
an activity or out of pure curiosity. 

•  People’s  motivations  for  tracking  guide  the  selection  of 
tracking  tools.  Behavior-change  trackers  do  substantial 
background  research  and  optimize  a  tool  for  their  goal, 
instrumental  trackers  try  to  maximize  their  benefit  from 
the  tools,  and  curiosity-driven  trackers  often  do  not 
actively consider tool selection. 

731

 
 
 
 
 •  Usage of tools varies by domain and reason for tracking, 
primarily  because  people’s  motivations  are  different  in 
each domain. Behavior change trackers collect and review 
data  frequently  and  are  bothered  by  inaccurate  logs, 
instrumental trackers track when the benefits outweigh the 
effort, and curiosity-driven trackers are motivated by how 
interesting they find the data they are tracking. 

•  People frequently switch tools, and in doing so learn about 

what and how they are interested in tracking.  

•  People  lapse  in  their  tool  use  in  four  different  ways: 
track,  difficulty  managing  upkeep, 

forgetting 
intentionally skipping entries, and suspending tracking. 

to 

These  findings  inform  a  revised  model  of  how  people  use 
self-tracking  tools,  independent  of  original  motivation, 
supplementing  the  stage-based  model  for  behavior-change 
tracking. This informs a set of open design problems: 
•  Designing to resume tracking after a lapse, including how 
to use historical data collected before, or even during, the 
lapse.  For  example,  should  a  food  journal  present 
someone’s  previous  eating  habits  when  they  decide  to 
resume tracking to aid in weight loss? If so, how? 

•  Designing to avoid inertia around a long-used tool that no 
longer  supports  a  self-tracker’s  goal.  For  example,  how 
can a financial tracking tool integrate historical data and 
provide an intuitive interface to a person who has managed 
their finances with a custom spreadsheet for years? 

•  Designing to support people’s various goals and transitions 
between  them.  How can the design of a location-tracking 
application  like  Foursquare  support  someone  who,  upon 
receiving a badge for frequent check-ins, becomes curious 
or  even  concerned  about  how  much  they  spend  at 
restaurants?  Can  a  location  diary  like  Moves  show 
someone who tries the application out of curiosity how it 
can help them achieve their fitness goals? 

BACKGROUND 
This  section  reviews  prior  work  on  models  of  personal 
informatics  use,  research  examining  how  people  use  tools, 
and how that use changes over time. 

Models of Personal Informatics 
Li et al. develop a stage-based model of personal informatics 
systems  and  describe  barriers  people  face  in  each  stage 
(Figure 1) [21]. The model has five stages: 1) preparation, or 
starting to collect data and thinking about what information 
to  record  and  how  to  collect  it;  2)  collection,  or  gathering 
data;  3)  integration,  or  preparing  data  to  reflect  upon; 
4) reflection, or examining and exploring data; and 5) action, 
or  taking  one’s  newfound  understanding  of  themselves  to 
inform changes. Motivated by the Transtheoretical Model of 
Behavior  Change  (TTM)  [32],  Li  et  al.  later  identify  two 
phases  of  reflection,  discovery  and  maintenance,  and  note 
people ask different types of questions in each phase [22]. 

From  studying  practices  of  the  Quantified  Self  movement, 
Choe  et  al.  learn  that  reflection  often  occurs  when  data  is 
captured  [6]  and  propose  a  new  model  for  self-monitoring

args.code:
import json

# Use locals() to access variables with special characters in names
papers_file_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:125']
citations_file_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:100']

print("Papers file path:", papers_file_path)
print("Citations file path:", citations_file_path)

# Load papers data
with open(papers_file_path, 'r') as f:
    all_papers = json.load(f)

# Load citations data  
with open(citations_file_path, 'r') as f:
    all_citations = json.load(f)

print('Loaded {} papers and {} citation records'.format(len(all_papers), len(all_citations)))

# Create citation lookup dictionary
citation_lookup = {}
for record in all_citations:
    citation_lookup[record['title']] = int(record['total_citations'])

# Find 2016 physical activity papers
papers_2016_pa = []
for paper in all_papers:
    filename = paper.get('filename', '')
    text = paper.get('text', '')
    
    if not filename:
        continue
    
    # Check if from 2016
    if '2016' not in text:
        continue
    
    title = filename.replace('.txt', '')
    text_lower = text.lower()
    title_lower = title.lower()
    
    # Physical activity keywords
    keywords = ['physical activity', 'fitness', 'exercise', 'workout']
    
    # Check if in title
    in_title = any(k in title_lower for k in keywords)
    
    # Count occurrences in text
    keyword_count = sum(text_lower.count(k) for k in keywords)
    
    if in_title or keyword_count >= 3:
        total_citations = citation_lookup.get(title, 0)
        papers_2016_pa.append({'title': title, 'total_citations': total_citations})

# Sort by citations descending
papers_2016_pa.sort(key=lambda x: x['total_citations'], reverse=True)

print('Found {} papers from 2016 in physical activity domain'.format(len(papers_2016_pa)))

# Show first few results
for i, paper in enumerate(papers_2016_pa[:5]):
    print('{}. {} - {} citations'.format(i+1, paper['title'][:60], paper['total_citations']))

# Generate output text
output = []
output.append('Papers from 2016 in Physical Activity Domain')
output.append('=' * 60)
output.append('Total papers found: {}'.format(len(papers_2016_pa)))
output.append('')

for paper in papers_2016_pa:
    output.append('Title: {}'.format(paper['title']))
    output.append('Total Citations: {}'.format(paper['total_citations']))
    output.append('')

result_text = '\n'.join(output)
print('__RESULT__:')
print(result_text)
